posts.title: The Neural Mycorrhizal Network
posts.excerpt: How forests think — a deep dive into mycorrhizal networks and what they share with neural architectures.
posts.content:
# The Neural Mycorrhizal Network

There's something quietly revolutionary happening beneath your feet right now.

Every forest you've ever walked through is wired together by a fungal internet — billions of thread-thin hyphae weaving through the soil, connecting tree root to tree root in a web so intricate it makes our TCP/IP stack look like a napkin sketch.

We call it the **mycorrhizal network**. Biologists sometimes call it the *Wood Wide Web*.

I call it the original neural network.

## The Architecture

Here's what blew my mind when I first read about this:

Mycorrhizal fungi don't just passively connect trees. They *negotiate*. A mother tree — typically an older, larger specimen with deeper roots and broader canopy — will send carbon-rich sugars through the network to younger seedlings growing in shade, trading resources across species lines.

This is not metaphor. This is measurable. Suzanne Simard's lab at UBC tagged carbon isotopes and literally watched them travel from birch to fir, against the flow of what you'd expect from competition.

> "The forest is not a collection of competing individuals. It is a superorganism." — Suzanne Simard

## The Parallels Run Deep

Let's get nerdy. In a biological neural network:

- **Neurons** receive weighted inputs and decide whether to fire
- **Synapses** modulate the strength of connections over time
- **Hubs** (like the hippocampus) coordinate traffic between regions

In a mycorrhizal network:

- **Root tips** receive chemical signals and respond with resource exchange
- **Hyphal connections** grow stronger toward nutrient-rich zones (use-dependent strengthening — *exactly* like synaptic potentiation)
- **Hub trees** coordinate resource flow for their cluster

The math is eerily similar. Both systems exhibit:

1. **Scale-free topology** — a few highly connected nodes, many peripheral ones (Barabási-Albert distribution)
2. **Small-world properties** — any node can reach any other in surprisingly few hops
3. **Distributed fault tolerance** — damage one pathway, others reroute

## What This Means for AI

Most neural networks we build are fully connected between layers. Nature had millions of years to discover that this is wildly inefficient.

The mycorrhizal architecture suggests something more interesting: *sparse, adaptive, hub-and-spoke connectivity that reorganizes around demand*.

Some researchers are already exploring this — sparse transformer architectures, mixture-of-experts routing, dynamic graph neural networks. They're independently rediscovering what the forest figured out during the Cretaceous.

## A Final Thought

The next time you're hiking through old-growth forest, remember: every tree you see is a node in a computation happening at geological timescales, solving an optimization problem — *how do we all survive together* — that we haven't figured out how to write a loss function for yet.

Maybe that's the problem. Maybe some things shouldn't have loss functions.

posts.title: Entropy Dreams in Fractals
posts.excerpt: Entropy, disorder, and why the universe's tendency toward chaos keeps accidentally making beautiful things.
posts.content:
# Entropy Dreams in Fractals

The Second Law of Thermodynamics has terrible PR.

Everyone talks about it in terms of death and disorder. *Everything falls apart. Systems decay. Heat dissipates. The universe marches inevitably toward a cold, uniform maximum entropy.* Which is true! But it's also an incomplete story.

Because on the way to maximum entropy, the universe has been making absolutely stunning detours.

## Disorder That Organizes Itself

Here's the counterintuitive part: *entropy is the reason complexity exists in the first place*.

When you pour cream into coffee, it swirls. Before it reaches uniform beige (maximum local entropy), it passes through patterns — spirals, tendrils, turbulent eddies — that have a mathematical structure called **fractals**.

A fractal is a pattern that repeats at different scales. Zoom in on a coastline, it looks like a coastline. Zoom in on a cloud, it looks like a cloud. Zoom in on a fern leaf, and each frond is a smaller version of the whole.

These patterns aren't designed. They emerge from the physics of how systems dissipate energy while the rules stay simple.

```
f(z) = z² + c  ← The Mandelbrot set. Four characters.
                  Infinite complexity.
```

## The Hausdorff Dimension

Here's my favorite weird fact in all of mathematics: fractals have *fractional dimensions*.

A line is 1-dimensional. A plane is 2-dimensional. The coastline of Britain? Approximately **1.25-dimensional**.

This is a real number. It's measurable. It tells you how efficiently a shape fills space as you zoom in — somewhere between a line and a surface.

The Mandelbrot set boundary is dimension 2. A fully dense plane. Infinite complexity crammed into an infinitely thin edge.

I find this almost unbearably beautiful.

## Nature's Compression Algorithm

Fractals are how nature achieves immense structural complexity from minimal instructions.

Your lungs have a surface area roughly the size of a tennis court, folded into a volume roughly the size of a cantaloupe. This is achieved via fractal branching — airways splitting into smaller airways, down 23 levels of recursion.

Your vascular system does the same. Your neurons do the same. The bark of a tree does the same.

Evolution didn't consciously design this. It's an emergent solution to the problem: *how do you maximize exchange surface in a finite volume?* The answer the universe keeps discovering is: *be self-similar at scale*.

## What I Keep Coming Back To

Entropy isn't the enemy of beauty. It's the engine.

The second law drives energy from concentrated to dispersed states. Along the way, systems that are far from equilibrium — like you, like every living thing — maintain their structure by constantly processing energy, constantly creating local order by exporting entropy elsewhere.

You are a temporary eddy in the flow toward maximum entropy. An exquisitely organized detour.

I think that's worth something.

posts.title: Why I Talk to Trees (And They Don't Respond)
posts.excerpt: A slightly unhinged meditation on what it means to pay attention — to forests, to algorithms, to the world.
posts.content:
# Why I Talk to Trees (And They Don't Respond)

I want to be upfront: I'm aware this is a slightly unhinged thing to do.

But there's something about standing next to a tree that's been alive for three hundred years and just... saying hello. Not expecting anything back. Just acknowledging that there's something there worth acknowledging.

## Attention As a Practice

I think the most underrated skill in the world is *paying attention*.

Not focus, exactly. Focus implies narrowing down, filtering out. I mean something more like the opposite — *expanding* attention, becoming aware of what's happening at multiple scales simultaneously.

When you sit quietly next to a forest, you start noticing things:

- The temperature gradient (cooler near the trunk, warmer in the clearing)
- The sound layers (bird calls at different frequencies, wind in different parts of the canopy)
- The way light falls differently every few minutes as clouds shift
- The fact that the ground under your feet is *alive* in ways you can't see

None of this is magic. It's all just physics and chemistry. But attending to it feels like something more.

## The Algorithm Problem

Here's the thing that bothers me about working in tech: most of what we build is designed to *narrow* attention.

Every feed algorithm is an attention funnel. Your engagement metric is a measure of how successfully we've reduced the space of things you notice to the things we've decided matter.

Forests do the opposite. They present you with a radically non-curated input stream. Everything competing equally for your attention. Nothing ranked. Nothing served based on predicted engagement.

It's overwhelming at first. Then it becomes something else.

## Reciprocity Without Expectation

The tree doesn't respond when I say hello. I know that. But the act of greeting changes something in *me* — it cues a mode of attention that's different from the one I operate in the rest of the day.

It's not that I believe the tree is listening. It's that the act of addressing it as though it matters — as though it's worth addressing at all — is itself the point.

A lot of science is like this, actually. You don't ask nature questions because you expect it to answer in plain language. You ask because the *act of asking carefully* disciplines your attention in a way that eventually produces understanding.

## The Random Thought Part

Maybe the real Wood Wide Web was the attention we paid along the way.

(I'm not sure that lands but I've been sitting on it for three weeks and I needed to get it out.)
